title: Talking clothes get our measure
content:
Can you imagine trousers that talk you into buying them?

Customers in a top international clothing store may soon find their prospective purchases telling them whether they would be a good fit. The system, which can be applied to jackets, skirts and almost any other garment, is seen as the most exciting innovation in retailing in years. It could cut out hours spent trying on clothes that will never fit and, once perfected, could mean the end of the changing room.

Any item of clothing, from a top-of-the-range suit to underwear, could be programmed to chat to its buyer, with warnings such as this is nice but not quite right for you, or encouragements aong the lines of 'I'm a perfect fit, or 'It suits you, sir.’

A version of the technology is already being worked on by a leading clothes retailer, whose next big sizing survey, the first in more than 10 years, will make use of the latest three-dimensional scanning technology. The shop will use the information to determine the shapes of its future clothing and it will also run a trial in which selected shoppers can use the data collected from the scanner to order clothes.

Customers who agree to take part in this pilot scheme will be led to a special booth by a shop assistant, who will ask them to strip off and stand still while intense beams of white light are played over their bodies. A computerised scanner will turn the results into a ‘virtual reflection' - an electronic recording of their exact shape.

The technology has already been taken further in the United States. Smart cards holding an individual's scan details are being designed to plug into a portable device that shoppers carry round the store with them. This enables people to enjoy their usual browsing experience.

As they pass racks of clothing in the store, tags on the clothes programmed with a selection of pre-recorded responses interact with the device and talk to the customer. They will be able to advise on the item of clothing's likely fit, or otherwise. For some customers, the prospect of successive pairs of trousers - in sizes that once might have fitted - loudly announcing that they are far too small could turn shopping into a humiliation. But the system is likely to be designed with these people in mind.

Instead of loudspeakers, customers can opt for a small earpiece, so that their potential trousers, suit or even underwear would not need to talk. They could just whisper softly in the ear and avoid any potential embarrassment. Stephen Gray, an expert in computer clothing research, explains that it will be possible for shops to allow customers to use their smart cards to order made-to-measure clothing. 'Translating three-dimensional images into two-dimensional clothing patterns is a skill we have lost as traditional tailoring has disappeared,' said Gray.

Interestingly, clothing manufacturers are extremely interested in the new possibilities this offers them. They are going to have to relearn the skills associated with making clothes by hand and then find a way to automate them. A challenge they are willing to take on in order to stay in business. Both clothing retailers and manufacturers are keen to be involved with this new technology. They are always looking for ways to give them the edge over their competitors and increase their market share. Those businesses which are among the first to embrace this technology may well attract new customers, especially those drawn to innovative technology.

However, they must remember that the average customer is extremely sophisticated these days. They will not just buy clothes because of the novel technology involved; the goods will have to be stylish, high quality and good value for money.

If this technology takes off, it will be interesting to see if it is taken up by other sectors, such as the food industry. In the future, could food tell us its nutritional value and whether it is suitable for us and how much of it we should consume per portion? This could be extremely useful for people who are allergic to nuts or wheat, for instance. 'Talking technology' is also of course enormously helpful to people who are visually impaired. As a result, several disabilities groups have also expressed interest in the technology and are looking into how it could make shopping easier for their members. Internet shopping, talking labels and spoken or videoed cooking instructions could revolutionise our lives.

questions.question_text: What is the primary focus of the passage?

options.text: The historical decline of traditional tailoring and its impact on modern fashion.
The psychological challenges customers face when trying on clothes in public booths.
The introduction of interactive technology to improve the shopping experience and garment fit.
A comparison between the retail industries in the United States and the United Kingdom.

questions.explanation: Bài viết chủ yếu thảo luận về việc sử dụng máy quét 3D và công nghệ 'trò chuyện' để giúp khách hàng chọn quần áo vừa vặn hơn, từ đó cải thiện trải nghiệm mua hàng.

questions.question_text: According to the pilot scheme described, how is a "virtual reflection" of a customer created?

options.text: By having a shop assistant manually input the customer’s measurements into a smart card.
Through the use of powerful light beams that record the individual's precise body shape.
By asking customers to try on a series of electronic garments in a changing room.
By analyzing the customer’s past shopping data and size history on a computer.

questions.explanation: Văn bản nói rằng 'intense beams of white light' sẽ được sử dụng để ghi lại hình dạng cơ thể chính xác của khách hàng, và một máy quét được lập trình sẽ chuyển đổi những kết quả đó thành một 'hình ảnh ảo'.

questions.question_text: Why are clothing manufacturers prepared to relearn traditional hand-making skills?

options.text: Because they want to reduce the cost of mass-producing low-quality garments.
To ensure they can maintain their market position and remain commercially viable.
Because there is a lack of advanced machinery available for 3D scanning.
To satisfy the demands of expert computer researchers like Stephen Gray.

questions.explanation: Bài viết đề cập rằng các nhà sản xuất sẵn sàng đối mặt với thách thức này 'để duy trì hoạt động kinh doanh' và tạo ra lợi thế so với đối thủ.

questions.question_text: In the sentence "But the system is likely to be designed with these people in mind," who does the phrase "these people" refer to?

options.text: Retailers who are looking for an advantage over their competitors.
Disabled groups who require spoken or videoed instructions.
Shoppers who might feel distressed if their clothing size is revealed publicly.
Experts who specialize in translating 3D images into 2D patterns.

questions.explanation: Câu trước thảo luận về việc mặc quần áo công khai thông báo kích thước 'quá nhỏ' có thể là một sự sỉ nhục; 'những người này' chỉ những người sẽ thấy trải nghiệm đó khó chịu.

questions.question_text: What can be inferred from the final part of the text regarding "talking technology"?

options.text: Its benefits are expected to extend beyond the fashion industry to improve safety and accessibility.
It has already replaced traditional labeling in most international food supermarkets.
The technology is primarily intended to help people lose weight by monitoring food intake.
Most sophisticated customers believe the technology is a gimmick and will not use it.

questions.explanation: Văn bản thảo luận về các ứng dụng tiềm năng trong ngành thực phẩm (an toàn cho dị ứng) và lợi ích cho người khiếm thị và nhóm người khuyết tật (khả năng tiếp cận).
